title: Finding David

jokes.text: The frantic bag identification exchange: 'My brown bag!' / 'What brown bag?' / 'You had a room full of brown bags back home, which brown bag?!'
jokes.setup: Moira panics about a missing bag, demanding Johnny take it seriously
jokes.punchline: Johnny's exasperated point that she owned so many bags the question is unanswerable deflates the drama
jokes.explanation: Opens the episode with strong character-specific comedy — the absurd excess of the Roses' former wealth (a room full of brown bags) grounds Moira's hysteria and Johnny's weary practicality simultaneously. Good economy in the rapid-fire exchange.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Has anyone seen my brown bag? My favorite brown bag?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, you have seventeen brown bags. You have brown bags in colors that brown hasn't even been invented yet.

jokes.text: 'That's an ostrich bag.'
jokes.setup: Moira has been dramatically lamenting her missing crocodile bag; Johnny says it's in the closet next to his briefcase
jokes.punchline: After all that panic, Johnny's flat correction that she's looking at the wrong exotic-skin bag entirely
jokes.explanation: A beautifully economical punchline — four words that do enormous work. It simultaneously reveals Moira has so many exotic-skin bags she can't keep track, deflates her drama entirely, and characterizes Johnny as the calm straight man. The specificity (ostrich vs. crocodile) elevates it above a generic 'wrong item' joke. Earns its high economy score.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It's a crocodile bag. A real crocodile bag.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's not a crocodile bag, Moira. It's an alligator bag.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, what's the difference?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: One is a crocodile and one is an alligator.

jokes.text: 'David must've taken it, along with my last molecule of hope!'
jokes.setup: Moira is told to calm down and breathe; she seems to be settling
jokes.punchline: She pivots immediately to blaming David and catastrophizing about losing her 'last molecule of hope'
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical flair — leaping from a missing bag to existential despair — is pure character comedy. 'Last molecule of hope' is a great phrase, pseudo-scientific and melodramatic at once. High character integration because this exact phrasing is distinctly Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: My bag! My beautiful, Italian leather bag! Gone. Just like my dignity, my fortune, and my will to live.

jokes.text: 'I bet he's pawned it by now, to pay for manicures.'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks what possible sense it would make for David to steal Moira's bag
jokes.punchline: Moira's specific accusation — pawning for manicures — is a hilariously targeted character assassination of David
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'manicures' is the joke — it's a perfectly chosen detail that reveals both Moira's low opinion of David's vices and David's established character. Economy is excellent; the line doesn't oversell itself.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David would never steal. He doesn't have the physical dexterity for it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Besides, if he had that kind of money, he'd spend it on something practical. Like a black turtleneck. Or another black turtleneck.

jokes.text: Johnny mid-sentence switches from 'a brown bag, as a matter of fact' to 'Well... our son, yes.' when Stevie asks what they're looking for
jokes.setup: Johnny is listing the bag as their priority to Stevie; he catches himself mid-answer
jokes.punchline: He belatedly remembers their SON is also missing and corrects course with sheepish 'Well... our son, yes'
jokes.explanation: A well-structured comedic beat — the Roses' warped priorities (bag over missing child) is a running theme for the episode, and this is its first clear articulation. Johnny's self-correction lands the joke cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You've been in that robe all day.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I have not been in this robe all day. I've been in several robes.

jokes.text: 'It doesn't... oh towels, great.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is logically walking through why David wouldn't steal the bag; Moira is about to concede the point
jokes.punchline: She interrupts her own admission of being wrong by pivoting to sarcastic acknowledgment of Stevie arriving with towels — luxury-deprived ennui as comedy
jokes.explanation: A small but effective character beat — Moira abandoning logical concession the moment she's distracted by her own suffering. The sarcasm about towels condenses her entire fish-out-of-water status into two words.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Stevie, I really appreciate you helping us look for David. I know you two are... close.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, he's important to me.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, I'm aware. Very... intimately aware.

jokes.text: Johnny describing Stevie and David's relationship as 'intimate' — Stevie immediately shuts down and pivots to towels
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to acknowledge that Stevie and David had a relationship, searching for the right word
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat 'Okay, I just came to drop off the towels' — a perfect retreat from an awkward overstep
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in Stevie's absolute refusal to engage with Johnny's clumsy attempt at emotional acknowledgment. Her retreat to the mundane (towels) is a classic deadpan escape from cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Has anyone seen David? He's been gone for like three hours and he didn't tell me where he was going.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David is a grown man, Alexis. He doesn't need to report his whereabouts to you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, but he always tells me. Stevie, you haven't heard from him?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No, not since this morning.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm sure he's fine. He probably just needed some air.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, but like, has anyone seen my good hair clip? Because I can't find it anywhere.

jokes.text: 'I'm still here!' — Moira calling from the bathroom, having heard Stevie's name
jokes.setup: Stevie was about to leave; Moira calls out as if she'd been forgotten in another room the whole time
jokes.punchline: 'I'm still here!' — her immediate pivot to asking Stevie for extra bedspreads makes clear her only interest is requests, not connection
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira self-importance — she makes her presence known only to make a demand. The beat where Johnny has to call her in and she IMMEDIATELY asks about bedspreads completes the joke well.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm here!

jokes.text: Alexis has pushed her bed to take over more floor space; 'Just giving myself some more space. There's a lot going on in my life right now.'
jokes.setup: Johnny enters Alexis's room in the motel and is clearly alarmed by something
jokes.punchline: Alexis has physically rearranged the tiny motel room to 'give herself more space' — solving a spatial problem with characteristic obliviousness to the absurdity
jokes.explanation: A strong visual gag that's deeply character-specific — Alexis treats a tiny motel room like her old mansion, rearranging furniture as a coping mechanism for her emotional chaos. 'There's a lot going on in my life' as justification for physically monopolizing shared space is perfectly Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I need more space in this relationship.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: More space? Alexis, I proposed to you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, and that was like, harassment.

jokes.text: Alexis's list of stressors: Ted's marriage proposal AND 'the whole Mutt issue, which is very complicated, and sexy'
jokes.setup: Alexis is explaining why she needed more space — she has 'a lot going on'
jokes.punchline: '...which is very complicated, and sexy' — the 'sexy' addition delivered with complete sincerity is the joke
jokes.explanation: The rhythm of 'complicated, and sexy' is perfect — a beat pause before 'sexy' that makes it land. This is pure Alexis voice: she genuinely cannot separate romantic chaos from its titillating appeal. High character integration and economy scores are earned.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm so sorry for your loss.

jokes.text: 'Well, I can't find him, so what would you say he is?' — Alexis responding to being told David is 'missing'
jokes.setup: Johnny implies that 'missing' is a dramatic way to put it; Alexis has just admitted she hasn't heard from David
jokes.punchline: Alexis's logic-trap: if she can't find him, the definition 'missing' is technically accurate, delivered with breezy nonchalance
jokes.explanation: A tidy little argumentative reversal — Alexis catches herself mid-dismissal and is technically correct, which makes her nonchalance funnier. Good structure and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, David's idea of teenage rebellion involved private jets.

jokes.text: Stevie to Johnny after his outburst: 'Well, I'm sorry for your loss.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has overreacted and snapped at Stevie for speaking plainly; David is technically just 'missing,' not dead
jokes.punchline: Stevie treats it like a bereavement — 'I'm sorry for your loss' — delivered completely deadpan
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stevie deadpan. Five words doing maximum work — simultaneously mock-consoling, mildly insulting, and exiting the conversation cleanly. The inappropriateness of funereal language for a missing (not dead) person is the joke, and Stevie's absolute commitment sells it. High economy score is very much earned.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David will be fine. He has an eye for luxury goods. I'm sure he'll find a way to... fence them.

jokes.text: 'It's not the first time David's run away, remember all those troubled years in high school, when he'd charter the jet without permission?'
jokes.setup: Johnny is nervous about not having found David; Moira tries to reassure him by citing precedent
jokes.punchline: The 'troubled' precedent she cites is chartering a private jet — a wealthy-family's completely skewed definition of 'troubled'
jokes.explanation: This joke captures Schitt's Creek's core satirical premise beautifully — the Roses' sense of 'troubled' is so divorced from normal reality that their version of teenage rebellion is unauthorized jet charters. High character integration because only Moira would frame this as a parenting anecdote with zero self-awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: This bag is a family heirloom. It's been passed down through generations of Rose women, each one using it for the exact same purpose.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What purpose?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Escaping marriages.

jokes.text: 'If there's one thing David knows, it's the street value of a woman's bag.'
jokes.setup: Johnny worries David has no money; Moira says 'oh, he does now'
jokes.punchline: Her confidence that David has intimate knowledge of handbag black-market pricing is the punchline — a backhanded compliment that's really just an insult
jokes.explanation: Excellent line. The phrase 'street value of a woman's bag' is a gloriously specific construction — combining luxury fashion knowledge with criminal underworld vocabulary. It characterizes both Moira (fashionista) and David (savvy, vanity-adjacent) with extreme economy. Genuinely quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I will never need an escape bag.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You're right, you won't.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, however...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh, she'll need multiple.

jokes.text: Okay, you know what, can we stop talking about that bag for five minutes, the bag, the bag, and the bag!
jokes.setup: Moira keeps returning to the bag as a co-equal concern to David's disappearance
jokes.punchline: Johnny's exasperated repetition of 'the bag, the bag, and the bag' captures his exhausted frustration — three mentions in one sentence
jokes.explanation: Functional frustrated-dad comedy. The triple repetition is a classic comedic device but it's not especially well-crafted here — it's more outburst than wit.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: I shouldn't come in.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: I'm already inside uninvited.

jokes.text: The revelation that Moira's bag is a family heirloom passed down as 'emergency currency in case we need to leave our husbands in the middle of the night'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks Moira to stop talking about the bag; she defends its sentimental value with its family history
jokes.punchline: The matrilineal tradition is specifically for funding midnight escapes from husbands — making it a dark commentary on the Rose women's relationship with marriage
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best sustained comedic revelation — it lands on multiple levels simultaneously. It's darkly funny (generations of women planning escape routes from their marriages), character-revealing (Moira's lineage as context for her theatrical nature), and has a perfect escalating structure. Johnny's response 'Oh, well, that's reassuring' is the ideal straight-man button.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: My family is going through some really complicated stuff right now.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Yeah, I heard. That's rough.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, we lost everything. Our money, our house, our staff...

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Mm-hmm.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So, I was wondering if maybe you could—

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: I'm not gonna be able to help you with that.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Oh, well, that's reassuring.' — after learning the bag is escape-from-husband currency
jokes.setup: Moira has just revealed the bag's purpose is to fund leaving one's husband
jokes.punchline: Johnny's dry 'Oh, well, that's reassuring' — understating his alarm with perfect comic timing
jokes.explanation: The button on joke #14 — Johnny's deadpan response completes the joke. It's the straight man's job and he does it with perfect economy. The rewatch bonus is because it lands harder when you see how perfectly Johnny underplays genuine marital anxiety.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: My parents have completely lost control of their lives. My mother hasn't had a facial in weeks, and my father's been wearing the same Tom Ford suit two days in a row.

jokes.text: Moira: 'You don't have to worry darling, I'll never need it. But you know Alexis will! Yeah, more than once, probably.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is rattled by the bag's purpose; Moira reassures him
jokes.punchline: Her reassurance immediately pivots to confidently predicting Alexis will need to flee multiple husbands — with Johnny's resigned 'yeah, more than once, probably'
jokes.explanation: Beautifully executed: Moira's self-exemption is the first laugh, but Johnny immediately co-signing the Alexis prediction ('yeah, more than once, probably') is the second and bigger laugh. Both parents casually predicting their daughter's chaotic romantic future with complete resignation is darkly funny and character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Who told you that we were together?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I did.

jokes.text: Moira anthropomorphizes the bag: 'And now John, it's out there, frightened, and alone...'
jokes.setup: Moira has been talking about the bag as an heirloom
jokes.punchline: She assigns the bag human emotional states — 'frightened and alone' — completely earnestly
jokes.explanation: Moira's theatrical excess reaches its logical extreme — the bag is no longer an object but a frightened soul in need of rescue. The joke is in her complete sincerity and Johnny's patient 'Okay, okay, we'll find your bag.' High character integration because this level of dramatic projection is uniquely Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Your hands are so soft! How is that possible when you do all this manual labor?

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: I use a lot of moisturizer.

jokes.text: Mutt enters having let himself into Alexis's room; 'Actually, you're standing in my living room, you let yourself in.'
jokes.setup: Mutt says he 'really shouldn't come in' — but he's already inside
jokes.punchline: Alexis points out the contradiction flatly — he's already IN her 'living room' (a motel room)
jokes.explanation: A small but functional joke — the absurdity of calling a motel room a 'living room' and catching Mutt's social awkwardness mid-sentence. Light but works.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh no, what did you steal now?

jokes.text: Alexis describes her mother as being in a 'waking coma' and her dad as having 'developed more of a stutter' as reasons their romantic situation is complicated
jokes.setup: Alexis is explaining why she can't simply be with Mutt
jokes.punchline: The reasons are her parents' comedic deterioration — described with clinical detachment as though listing symptoms — rather than any genuine moral concern
jokes.explanation: 'Waking coma' is a funny coinage for Moira's state, and the clinical listing of parental symptoms as logistical obstacles (rather than reasons for sympathy) is quintessentially Alexis. Solid character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: The Rose family has real dignity and class.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Have you seen Moira in those wigs?

jokes.text: Alexis says Ted 'was misinformed' that things were done between them. Mutt: 'By who?' Alexis: 'By me.'
jokes.setup: Mutt assumes things are done with Ted since Alexis told him so
jokes.punchline: Alexis cheerfully admits she was the source of the misinformation — she lied to both of them simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Tight setup/punchline with excellent structure. The Q&A format (By who? / By me.) delivers the punchline with perfect rhythm. Alexis's complete lack of shame about being her own misinformation source is character-perfect. This is among the episode's most quotable moments — high economy, the whole joke in two words.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, he'll abandon me the moment he finds a cashmere sweater and a boy who knows how to make a cocktail.

jokes.text: I thought things were done with Ted. / Me too. / But apparently, he was misinformed. / By who? / By me.
jokes.setup: Mutt thought Alexis had ended things with Ted
jokes.punchline: Alexis confirms Ted was 'misinformed' — and that she was the one who misinformed him; she misled Ted about her intentions
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best constructed jokes. The four-beat exchange is textbook: setup (things were done), confirmation (me too), subversion (apparently he was misinformed), final twist (by me). The passive construction of 'he was misinformed' followed by the one-word self-indictment 'By me' is extremely economical and very funny. Pure Alexis — she casually confesses to misleading Ted as if it's a logistical detail. High quotability, strong structure.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, statistically speaking, most missing persons are found within the first 48 hours. After that, the odds drop significantly. So really, if we're being practical about it, we should probably call off the search.

jokes.text: Alexis complimenting Mutt's hands: 'Soft for someone who does nothing but manual labor.' Mutt: 'Thanks, I make sure to moisturize.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is resisting temptation; she's been distracted by Mutt's hands
jokes.punchline: Mutt's completely serious answer about moisturizing — a rugged outdoorsman who has an earnest skincare routine
jokes.explanation: A great subverted expectation — Mutt's rugged farm-worker exterior gives way to a moisturizer routine, and his completely earnest delivery of 'Thanks, I make sure to moisturize' is the joke. Alexis's 'Mm, that's so important. So important.' continuing the bit is a nice extension.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I found the truck! And David's with it!

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's wonderful!

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I found the truck.

jokes.text: Roland: 'What, are you here to tell me Alexis has skipped town with my lawn mower?!'
jokes.setup: The Roses arrive at Roland's door; Roland opens with an aggressive non-sequitur
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately assumes the Roses are bringing bad news, and his specific fear (Alexis + his lawn mower) is the joke — a perfectly absurd Roland-brain leap
jokes.explanation: Classic Roland — a genuine laugh at the specificity of his paranoia. The combination of Alexis (established as chaotic) and a lawn mower (absurdly mundane) is a Roland non-sequitur that feels character-authentic.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, if David was dead, somebody would've called us by now. That's how it works.

jokes.text: Jocelyn to Roland: 'Roland, these people have literally hit rock bottom, they're not in the mood for your jokes right now.' — then immediately adds 'She is.' pointing at Moira
jokes.setup: Jocelyn defends the Roses from Roland's joke — a rare moment of genuine empathy
jokes.punchline: She immediately exempts Moira, pointing at her — implying Moira is always in the mood for drama/jokes
jokes.explanation: A smart two-beat joke: the setup is Jocelyn defending the Roses, the punchline is the instant exception for Moira. The misdirection is clean — we expect general empathy, get a specific, accurate character read. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, I actually know about this. So my friend Mutt—well, not my friend Mutt, just this guy Mutt—anyway, he went missing for like three days, and it turned out he was just at a cabin. But like, a really weird cabin. And the guy who owned it? Total psychopath. He had like, teeth everywhere. Human teeth. And he kept them in jars. And one time he tried to—actually, you know what, I'm not gonna finish that story because it gets really dark, but basically he's in jail now, which is probably for the best.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh, I haven't lost him, I know exactly where he is. He's enjoying happy hour at the Viceroy without me.'
jokes.setup: Roland says he can't imagine the stress of losing a child
jokes.punchline: Moira insists she hasn't lost him — and her 'knowing exactly where he is' is a fantasy about David at a luxury hotel rather than genuine certainty
jokes.explanation: Peak Moira Rose — her coping mechanism for a missing son is a comforting fantasy that he's enjoying happy hour at a luxury resort. The specificity of 'the Viceroy' grounds it. And the 'without me' adds a note of wounded exclusion that's both funny and sad.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: You haven't been eating? Alexis, you just finished a whole rotisserie chicken in my office.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That was stress eating.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: You stress ate an entire chicken?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I stress eat.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Statistics tell us you have a 48 hour window before you can assume somebody is dead.' Johnny: 'Yeah, I just said it's been three days.'
jokes.setup: Roland is trying to be reassuring with dubious statistics about missing persons
jokes.punchline: Johnny's flat correction — Roland's reassurance is meaningless because the 48-hour window passed a day ago
jokes.explanation: Roland's obliviousness to the practical irrelevance of his reassurance is funny, and Johnny's weary one-liner delivery is perfect. The joke structure is clean and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I picture you at the bottom of a lake.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Why would you picture me at the bottom of a lake?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I don't know, it's just the first image that comes to mind.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, technically, it would be a quarry.

jokes.text: Roland's phone call — he spends a minute on pleasantries ('I'm good, how about you? Terrific.') before getting to the news that they found the truck
jokes.setup: Roland has gotten a call about the search; the Roses are waiting anxiously
jokes.punchline: Roland chit-chats at length while a family waits for news about their missing son
jokes.explanation: Roland's social obliviousness — his inability to read the room and prioritize the news over pleasantries — is a sustained cringe beat. Works as character comedy though it's not the sharpest joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I can't marry Ted because David left town, and if David can just leave whenever he wants, then what's stopping Ted from leaving me?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I didn't leave town.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You literally just said you were moving to California.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not leaving town, that's moving away for a new life opportunity.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Exactly. So Ted could do the same thing.

jokes.text: Roland reports they found the truck — 'And David.' / 'Well, that didn't come up.'
jokes.setup: Roland triumphantly announces they found the truck; the Roses wait for news of David
jokes.punchline: 'And David.' / 'Well, that didn't come up.' — Roland genuinely didn't think to ask about the son, only the truck
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest jokes. 'Well, that didn't come up' is perfectly constructed — five words that reveal Roland cares more about his truck than the missing person, and that he genuinely didn't think to ask. The beat where Johnny says 'Well didn't you ask?' — and Roland has to admit the son simply 'didn't come up' — is a great comic structure. High economy and structure scores are absolutely earned.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Darling, is this car a residence, or are you just transporting a residence?

jokes.text: Roland trying to reassure: 'Well I think they would've told me if there's a dead body with the truck, honey.'
jokes.setup: The Roses are alarmed that David wasn't mentioned in the truck report
jokes.punchline: Roland's version of reassurance is 'there probably isn't a dead body' — the comfort floor is catastrophically low
jokes.explanation: Roland's well-meaning but disastrously low bar for reassurance is classic character comedy. 'Honey' at the end makes it somehow worse — an added patronizing touch. The joke is in how thoroughly Roland fails to comfort while genuinely trying.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I would, but I'm getting a manicure and I already paid for it.

jokes.text: Alexis's non-sequitur story about her kindergarten friend who got adopted by a rich Asian family in Vancouver, who then had their own kid, and 'I can't remember whether she's back on the streets now...'
jokes.setup: The family is discussing David being missing and waiting for news
jokes.punchline: Alexis launches into a completely irrelevant story that trails off into uncertainty about her friend's current homelessness — a story that was supposed to be reassuring but becomes increasingly dark
jokes.explanation: Alexis's storytelling mode — a barely relevant anecdote that escalates into casual darkness — is one of the show's great running pleasures. 'I can't remember whether she's back on the streets now' as a throwaway trailing clause is the perfect Alexis landing. High character integration because this particular narrative style is uniquely hers.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, I've been kidnapped by pirates before, and I didn't make this big of a fuss about it.

jokes.text: Alexis pivoting from worry about David to 'And he took his hair straightener, which he knows that I use more than he does. This whole thing is just very selfish.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has been performing concern for David; her actual grievance emerges
jokes.punchline: Her real complaint about David's disappearance is the hair straightener — making 'selfish' the funniest word in the line
jokes.explanation: The joke hinges on Alexis calling David 'selfish' for taking a hair straightener when he's the one who disappeared — a perfect inversion of sympathy. It also reveals sibling rivalry as the true emotional driver under the concern, which is both funny and deeply character-true.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was in the middle of my microdermabrasion. I couldn't possibly interrupt that. One doesn't just stop mid-dermis.

jokes.text: Ted appears; Alexis has claimed she hasn't been sleeping or eating — 'You just ordered the parfait.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is performing distress for Ted's benefit — listing symptoms of her anguish
jokes.punchline: Ted's simple, factual contradiction: 'You just ordered the parfait.' — catching her performance with zero judgment
jokes.explanation: Five words, perfectly placed. 'You just ordered the parfait' is an exceptional comedy line — supremely economical, structurally elegant (the longer Alexis's lie, the better the punchline lands), and delivered with Ted's signature earnest obliviousness. One of the episode's most quotable lines. Economy score is nearly maxed — there isn't a single unnecessary word.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So the thing with Mutt is getting kind of complicated.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What do you mean complicated?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, we're kind of seeing each other.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're seeing Mutt? Mutt Schitt?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, but also kind of Ted.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're seeing Ted and Mutt at the same time?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Not at the same time, just... consecutively.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, that's not better. That's actually worse.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'And I just keep picturing him at the bottom of a lake.' Ted: 'Oh, no, that guy was like, a known alcoholic, and they pulled him out a week ago.'
jokes.setup: Alexis is catastrophizing about David; Ted responds to what he hears literally
jokes.punchline: Ted thinks Alexis is talking about an actual local drowning victim — the casual delivery of 'known alcoholic... pulled him out a week ago' as reassurance is darkly absurd
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed misunderstanding joke. Ted's literal interpretation of Alexis's metaphor yields the information that there was an actual person at the bottom of a lake (a known alcoholic). The small-town specificity ('they pulled him out a week ago') makes it land harder. Both characters are perfectly in-character — Alexis dramatic, Ted helpfully literal.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't really want to talk about this right now.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is that a bug?!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? No, that's—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Get it off! Get it off me!

jokes.text: Ted wondering if his texts aren't getting through — 'or maybe I'm sending them wrong?'
jokes.setup: Ted has been trying to reach Alexis and she hasn't been responding; he's looking for an explanation
jokes.punchline: Ted's generous self-blame — maybe HE'S been texting wrong — when the answer is clearly Alexis has been ignoring him
jokes.explanation: A gentle Ted joke — his chronic self-doubt and niceness is the comedic trait on display. Functional, character-consistent, but not among the sharpest.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I had to wake up at 4 AM to milk cows. Do you know what I was doing at 4 AM before that? Sleeping. In a bed. A very nice bed. With Egyptian cotton sheets.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And then I had to shovel manure. Actual manure. While wearing a scratchy wool shirt that made me look like I was auditioning for a depression-era documentary.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The closest I'd come to manual labor before that was pointing at things I wanted in a catalog.

jokes.text: Alexis's circular logic: 'Technically though, someone did leave, and so the dust just hasn't really settled on the whole... us leaving thing. Yet.'
jokes.setup: Ted points out she said she'd marry him if they didn't leave town, and they didn't leave
jokes.punchline: Alexis technically reframes David leaving as enough 'leaving' to create continued ambiguity — delivered with complete fake-casual sincerity
jokes.explanation: Alexis's creative logic — using David's disappearance as a technicality to evade a marriage commitment — is both infuriating and hilarious. The 'Yet' at the end is the topper, implying this ambiguity has no foreseeable end date.

dialogue.speaker: Amish man
dialogue.line: Well, it's been very nice having you stay with us. You've been a wonderful guest.

dialogue.speaker: Amish man
dialogue.line: But I have to say, I am so relieved that someone finally came to pick you up.

jokes.text: Ted asks if Alexis is scared 'about David, or about being with...' and Alexis replies 'Yeah, that.' — collapsing both fears into one non-answer
jokes.setup: Ted tries to give Alexis an emotional out — asking if her fear is about David
jokes.punchline: 'Yeah, that.' — Alexis eagerly accepts the wrong option of his either/or question, then the silence sits
jokes.explanation: Alexis's dodge is efficient and character-perfect — 'Yeah, that' as a two-word avoidance of an emotional question is her at her most Alexis. The comedy and the cringe are well-balanced.

dialogue.speaker: David Rose
dialogue.line: We learned humility, we learned to appreciate the simple things, and we learned that apparently Amish people can go to Costco.

jokes.text: Moira to Stevie in the car: 'Stevie dear, now don't take this the wrong way, but, is this car your home?'
jokes.setup: Stevie is driving the Roses to find David; Moira notices something about the car
jokes.punchline: Moira asks if the car is Stevie's home — an incredibly intrusive question prefaced by 'don't take this the wrong way,' which makes it worse
jokes.explanation: The 'don't take this the wrong way' prefix before an extremely offensive question is a great cringe setup. It's deeply Moira — both classist and delivered with genuine, oblivious concern. Stevie's flat 'Excuse me?' is the perfect beat.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not ready to leave.

dialogue.speaker: Amish man
dialogue.line: Well, you have to. Your time is up.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Oh yeah! Um, but there's donuts under the seat, those are still good.' — after Moira takes her sandwich
jokes.setup: Moira has taken Stevie's sandwich without permission
jokes.punchline: Stevie immediately volunteers the location of additional food with complete equanimity — not upset, just helpful
jokes.explanation: Stevie's unflappable response to Moira's entitlement — not upset, just redirecting to the donuts — is quietly funny. Her acceptance of the chaos around her is well-characterized.

dialogue.speaker: Amish man
dialogue.line: David, we will cherish the memory of you... and it will haunt us. Forever.

jokes.text: Alexis is told David has been found; she's asked to come. Her response: 'I'm just super tired from walking, though.'
jokes.setup: David has been missing three days; Alexis is told he's been found and urged to come
jokes.punchline: Alexis's response is fatigue from walking — a trivially petty obstacle to reuniting with her missing brother
jokes.explanation: Alexis's casual indifference to urgency — prioritizing her tired legs over reuniting with her missing brother — is a clean, economical character joke. Works well as a quick beat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was hoping you might offer us some traditional Amish hospitality. Perhaps a meal?

dialogue.speaker: Amish man
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Moira in the car: 'This is exactly what David wants. All of us worried, and driving around in some tin can looking for him. It's a tactic. It's subterfuge. He shouldn't have left me behind, that was never part of the plan.'
jokes.setup: The family is driving to retrieve David
jokes.punchline: Moira's theory that David's disappearance is a calculated tactic — and her specific grievance is that she wasn't included in the plan
jokes.explanation: This is a wonderfully Moira line — her interpretation of David's disappearance as a conspiracy he forgot to include her in is both hilarious and darkly revealing. 'That was never part of the plan' implies she and David have some kind of tacit escape-the-town pact. Maximum character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Nobody even cared that Alexis was missing. I mean, shouldn't someone have noticed? Shouldn't someone have called the police?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Actually, the FBI called me. Apparently I'm on some kind of federal watchlist.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? Why would you be on a federal watchlist?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I don't know, something about that time I accidentally brought a prohibited substance through customs? Or maybe it was the other time. Anyway, they check in on me regularly.

jokes.text: Alexis's Somali pirate hostage anecdote: taken hostage on David Geffen's yacht for a week, nobody answered her texts because 'I had just had my eyelashes dyed, everything was cloudy!'
jokes.setup: Moira and Johnny are called dramatic for worrying about David; Alexis defends herself by citing a worse situation where no one helped her
jokes.punchline: The reason no one responded to her hostage texts: freshly dyed eyelashes made it hard to see. The hostage situation is casually narrated; the eyelash detail is the absurd kicker
jokes.explanation: The standout joke of the episode and one of Schitt's Creek's great Alexis moments. Every element is calibrated perfectly: (1) being held hostage on DAVID GEFFEN'S yacht establishes the absurd wealth register; (2) Somali pirates — treated as a casual inconvenience; (3) the reason no one responded is that SHE had cloudy vision from eyelash dye, not anyone else's failure; (4) the explanation is delivered as if it fully justifies the non-response. This joke reveals Alexis's entire character history in one anecdote while being structurally impeccable. Extremely high character integration — this is ONLY Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I took the bag because Moira said it was a limited edition Birkin from a personal friend in Paris, but it's clearly a knockoff from a street vendor in Chinatown.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: It is NOT a knockoff. It's a homage.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It has a typo. It says 'Birkin' with two R's.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'David has spent the last three days on a farm. With the amish!' — as if this is the worst possible outcome
jokes.setup: The car has been speculating about where David is; Johnny has the answer
jokes.punchline: The horror in Johnny's delivery of 'with the amish' — as if Amish farm life is genuinely traumatic for someone like David
jokes.explanation: A solid situational joke — the horror is contextual (David, a glamorous city person with luxury tastes, on an Amish farm). The two-beat delivery — pause before 'with the amish' — implies the writers knew that was the funny word and placed it accordingly.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You stole my makeup in high school.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I didn't steal it. I was experimenting.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You were experimenting with my MAC lipstick?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It was a very good shade. I was exploring my identity.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'David has spent the last three days on a farm.' [pause] 'With the Amish!'
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to explain the situation simply
jokes.punchline: The Amish detail lands as a delayed revelation — as though 'farm' alone was not absurd enough for this family
jokes.explanation: The pause and the separate 'With the Amish!' is a good structural comedy beat. The Amish escalation is the right punchline for this family — the most tech-free, luxury-free environment conceivable for David Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I didn't lie about the bag. I simply didn't tell the truth. There's a difference.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And before you start with me about spreading celebrity gossip, that bag was a gift from a very famous person, and I would never betray their confidence by telling you who it was.

jokes.text: The family all yelling 'David!' at once; David yelling back 'Stop yelling David! You come here!'
jokes.setup: The family rushes toward David, all calling his name simultaneously
jokes.punchline: David reverses the power dynamic entirely — he's the one who's been missing for three days but he's the one issuing commands
jokes.explanation: David's reversal — from missing person to authority figure in the reunion — is a great character beat. His inability to accept concern gracefully manifests in redirecting the scene's energy immediately.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I orchestrated a dramatic exit, sweeping out of the room with purpose and intention, only to realize I'd left my phone, my wallet, and any semblance of dignity in the other room.

jokes.text: Moira's worried expression about David voiced as: 'Where's David, or his bags?'
jokes.setup: David teases Moira about her concern
jokes.punchline: Moira's supposed maternal concern is revealed to have always included — or possibly prioritized — the bag
jokes.explanation: A callback that pays off the episode's running bag obsession — Moira's self-quote ('Where's David, or his bags?') retroactively makes the bags equal in priority to the child. The callback earns a rewatch bonus because in retrospect Moira's emotional journey has always been about the bag as much as David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I had to sleep in a truck last night.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, really? Was it a nice truck?

jokes.text: Alexis to David: 'Okay, it's gotten more complicated! We slept together!' — blurting out the Mutt situation mid-family reunion
jokes.setup: David asks what's happening with Mutt; Alexis was supposed to keep this brief
jokes.punchline: Alexis blurts the full confession in a public family reunion setting; David's 'Ooh...' is the reaction beat
jokes.explanation: The timing is the joke — a private confession dropped in the middle of a chaotic family reunion. Functional cringe comedy, not particularly original but executed with good timing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Thank you for asking about me.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I didn't ask about you. I was hoping you'd stay gone.

jokes.text: 'What if I'm not ready to come home?!' / 'There's a bug on your dress.' / 'Ew!'
jokes.setup: David is making his dramatic stand about not being ready to leave the farm
jokes.punchline: Moira spots a bug on David's dress — his principle immediately collapses into 'Ew!' and flailing
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured deflation joke — David's dramatic last stand ('What if I'm not ready to come home?!') is immediately, completely neutralized by a bug on his dress. The structure is near-perfect: the more theatrical his resistance, the funnier the mundane interruption. High character integration: only David's specific combination of dramatics and fastidiousness makes this land.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'm back. And I have to say, despite the rather pointed lack of enthusiasm in your greeting, it is genuinely nice to be somewhere that feels... relatively warm.

jokes.text: Miriam makes the most delicious butter. Yeah, I mean, she starts pounding that cream about a half hour too early in the morning, but it tastes exactly like the butter we had at the Ritz in Paris, you know, that sort of... Saltless butter.
jokes.setup: David has apparently settled into Amish farm life; he gives his assessment of the experience
jokes.punchline: David's Amish farm experience is filtered entirely through luxury-hotel comparisons — the butter is judged against the Ritz in Paris; even the complaint (too early in the morning) is the complaint of a hotel guest, not a farm resident
jokes.explanation: This is wonderful character comedy — David has genuinely found a point of connection with Amish life (the butter) but can only frame it through his luxury-world reference points. 'That sort of... saltless butter' delivered as a refined critique is perfect David. The Ritz in Paris as the benchmark for Amish butter is absurdly specific in the best way. Very high character integration.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I've been thinking about what Stevie said, and I realize I'm not equipped to handle rejection. I don't have the social skills or the emotional maturity to process disappointment. I'm basically a man-child who's never had to face consequences, and now that I'm finally experiencing real life, I'm completely falling apart. It's pathetic, really.

jokes.text: Amish farmer's wife on David: 'We've spent two sleepless nights, wondering what would happen if nobody came. Would we be stuck with him?!'
jokes.setup: Moira is thanking the Amish family effusively for 'nurturing' David
jokes.punchline: The Amish woman's response reveals David was a burden — 'Would we be stuck with him?!' — flipping the expected gracious hosting dynamic
jokes.explanation: An excellent subversion — the Amish family's apparent hospitality is revealed as polite endurance. 'Would we be stuck with him?!' is the episode's other top-tier joke, because it confirms what we've been imagining: David Rose on an Amish farm was a mutual ordeal. The exclamation mark of genuine relief that anyone came for him is perfect. High earned vs. cheap — this pays off setup without shortcuts.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm realizing that Stevie was my one friend. My only friend. Which is... a lot of pressure to put on a person.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: And also explains why I'm so difficult.

jokes.text: Amish man listing what David's stay taught them: 'patience, forgiveness...' — another Amish man adds 'Restraint.'
jokes.setup: The Amish farmer is listing virtues David's stay reinforced
jokes.punchline: A second Amish man adds 'Restraint' — implying the restraint they had to exercise was not attacking David
jokes.explanation: Single-word punchline that does maximum work. 'Restraint' — delivered with complete deadpan — implies the entire story of David's time there without a word of exposition. A masterclass in comedic economy. The Amish deadpan sells it perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, is this me? I look like I've been through a wind tunnel. My face is so swollen, I look like I got stung by a thousand bees. I don't even recognize myself. How is anyone supposed to find me if I don't even look like me anymore?

jokes.text: Moira: 'Well it seems you've taken very good care of our possessions' — referring to David as a 'possession'
jokes.setup: Moira is thanking the Amish family
jokes.punchline: She thanks them for taking care of 'our possessions' — reducing David to property while trying to be gracious
jokes.explanation: Moira casually categorizing David as a possession — not a person — while delivering what she thinks is a warm thank-you is peak Moira obliviousness. Works as a quick, sharp character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, David, your vanity is not a virtue. It's charmless, really. Like watching someone admire themselves in a mirror that's slightly too close to their face.

jokes.text: Amish farmer to David: 'And yet your time with us has come to an end.' — pre-emptively ending David's protest
jokes.setup: David says he's not sure he's ready to come home
jokes.punchline: The farmer's firm but serene 'And yet your time with us has come to an end' — a polite but absolute eviction
jokes.explanation: The Amish farmer's serene certainty makes this a great deadpan line — it's simultaneously polite and non-negotiable. The gentle construction ('And yet...') is a perfect comedy vehicle for what is essentially 'Get out.'

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm not pressing charges.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, that's so nice of you! Did you hear that, David? He's not pressing charges!

jokes.text: David's farewell speech to the Amish family: 'Thank you for your kindness, when no one else showed me kindness. And compassion, when no one else showed...'
jokes.setup: David is saying goodbye to the Amish family — his family is standing right there
jokes.punchline: David's farewell speech is a passive-aggressive indictment of his own family, delivered to their faces
jokes.explanation: David's passive-aggression weaponized as a farewell speech is excellent character comedy. The speech is technically about thanking the Amish family but functions entirely as an accusation toward his own family.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Roland, I'm truly sorry for what I said. I hope you can forgive me.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Did you mean that?

jokes.text: Johnny interrupting David's speech to ask for Amish lunch: 'I imagine it's customary for strangers to partake in a good old fashioned amish lunch?' / 'Not always.' / 'Have a safe trip.'
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to convert the emotional farewell into an opportunity for free food
jokes.punchline: The farmer's flat 'Not always' / 'Have a safe trip' — the most efficient possible rejection of Johnny's gambit
jokes.explanation: Johnny's opportunistic lunch request mid-emotional-farewell is a great character beat — the Roses' obliviousness to social propriety is a core trait. The farmer's two-part rejection ('Not always' / 'Have a safe trip') is extremely economical.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Now add the chili flakes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Mom, we already did that like twenty minutes ago.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, I have no idea where we are.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'What you did was impulsive, capricious and melodramatic. But it was also wrong.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is lecturing David about stealing the truck; he's building toward a moral point
jokes.punchline: 'But it was also wrong.' — the adjectives (impulsive, capricious, melodramatic) are vivid but not moral; Johnny adds 'wrong' as an afterthought, as if it's a lesser point
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly structured line — the three descriptors before 'wrong' are all aesthetic judgments (impulsive, capricious, melodramatic), and 'wrong' lands as an afterthought because Johnny leads with the stylistic critique before the ethical one. The structure perfectly inverts what a normal parental lecture looks like.

jokes.text: 'Do you know how many times I've had to apologize to Roland? You can't just leave with other people's trucks, David!'
jokes.setup: David is being chastised for running away
jokes.punchline: Johnny's opening grievance isn't 'we were worried sick' but 'I've had to apologize to ROLAND' — implying repeated apologies to Roland Schitt is a specific and particularly painful punishment
jokes.explanation: Solid character comedy. 'Do you know how many times I've had to apologize to Roland?' captures the specific humiliation of a former CEO repeatedly groveling to Roland Schitt. The phrase 'how many times' implies this is an ongoing problem, which is funny.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, nobody freaked out when Alexis went missing!' Alexis: 'I didn't go missing, David. The FBI knew where I was the entire time.'
jokes.setup: David is arguing the family didn't worry enough about him; he uses Alexis going missing as a comparison
jokes.punchline: Alexis's correction — she didn't 'go missing,' the FBI was tracking her — simultaneously confirms a wild backstory and completely misses David's point
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines and a top-tier Alexis moment. 'The FBI knew where I was the entire time' is delivered as a perfectly sincere distinction — for Alexis, FBI surveillance is a meaningful difference from being 'missing.' The joke works on multiple levels: it implies a history of FBI involvement in Alexis's life, makes her sound both more worldly and more oblivious simultaneously, and is entirely character-specific. Very high quotability.

jokes.text: What bag? / You stole my bag, David. / That bag? That bag looks like it hasn't been used in a hundred years, so I put my toiletries in it.
jokes.setup: Johnny mentions they found Moira's bag; David is confused about which bag
jokes.punchline: David's defense — the bag looked old and unused, so he logically repurposed it as a toiletry bag — completely innocent from his perspective, devastating from Moira's
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the bag mystery — David didn't steal it for resale but simply pressed it into service as a toiletry bag, which might be worse. 'I put my toiletries in it' as the innocent explanation for the heirloom's use is excellent. The callback lands well after the full episode of bag-anxiety.

jokes.text: David: 'Did you come for me, or did you come for that bag?' — forcing the family to admit their mixed priorities
jokes.setup: The family has been arguing; the bag has been a recurring obsession
jokes.punchline: David's direct confrontation of the bag-vs-son priority question; 'We came for you!' overlapping with Moira 'This was my grandmother's bag!'
jokes.explanation: The bag running gag reaches its confrontational peak — David calls out what we've all been watching, and the family's overlapping responses ('We came for you!' / 'This was my grandmother's bag!') confirm exactly what David suspected. A satisfying callback payoff.

jokes.text: David: 'I hitchhiked, in the middle of the night, wearing all black! I was basically asking to get hit by a car!'
jokes.setup: David is making his case for how dramatic and dangerous his departure was
jokes.punchline: His self-aware acknowledgment that wearing all black while hitchhiking at night was self-destructive — delivered as a grievance rather than a confession
jokes.explanation: David's self-incrimination as argument — 'I was basically asking to get hit by a car!' — is a great David moment. He's simultaneously complaining about his treatment AND admitting his choices were terrible. High character integration because only David would frame near-self-destruction as evidence of how much he needed rescuing.

jokes.text: Moira: 'You told me that Diane Sawyer gave you that bag, and that it was fake. I didn't want you taking it.'
jokes.setup: Moira explains why she lied about the bag being from Diane Sawyer and fake
jokes.punchline: Moira's protective lie — she told David a prominent journalist gave her a fake bag — to hide the actual heirloom significance
jokes.explanation: The backstory joke — Moira's specific lie (Diane Sawyer + fake) to deter David — confirms both her craftiness and her inability to just have a normal conversation about valuables.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, because I have told a lot of people that Diane Sawyer sells fake bags on the down-low.'
jokes.setup: David learned the Diane Sawyer story was false; he has a follow-up concern
jokes.punchline: David has been spreading the rumor about Diane Sawyer selling counterfeit bags — and his primary concern is not the truth but the reputational damage he's caused a major journalist
jokes.explanation: A genuinely great joke with perfect character integration. The idea that David has been casually defaming Diane Sawyer for years — and his concern is about the misinformation he's spread, not any personal consequence — is both absurdist and deeply David. 'On the down-low' is the perfect specific phrase. Very high quotability.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I slept in the cab of a truck! Overnight!'  Alexis: 'Oh my God, who hasn't, David!'
jokes.setup: David is listing his sufferings — sleeping in a truck cab overnight
jokes.punchline: Alexis's 'Oh my God, who hasn't, David!' — delivered with genuine exasperation, implying truck-cab sleeping is a common experience in her life
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. 'Who hasn't, David!' is peak Alexis — her implying that truck-cab sleeping is a universal experience (for people like her) simultaneously reveals her chaotic history and punctures David's martyrdom with a single, perfectly delivered line. Maximum character integration and economy.

jokes.text: How are-yeah. / What? / Um... I come bearing gifts.
jokes.setup: David returns to Stevie's motel office having told her he had feelings for her before leaving
jokes.punchline: The awkward half-greeting — 'How are-yeah' abandoning mid-sentence — captures the specific discomfort of returning after an emotional declaration; the pivot to 'I come bearing gifts' is a social rescue attempt
jokes.explanation: The aborted greeting — 'How are-yeah' — is a lovely awkward beat. The failed social script of someone who doesn't know what to say after emotional vulnerability is well-observed.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'I think your car might need a bit of a clean. Is that a dead squirrel on your hood?'
jokes.setup: David has arrived at Stevie's office bearing gifts; she greets him
jokes.punchline: Stevie's deadpan observation about a dead squirrel on David's car hood — delivered as a casual aside after his emotional return
jokes.explanation: Stevie cuts through the emotional weight of David's return with a mundane practical observation. A classic Stevie deflation move. Visual gag works in context — the car has clearly seen better days.

jokes.text: David says people told him Stevie was calling around asking for him; Stevie says 'Oh, well, I didn't.' / 'They said that you were.' / 'Well, they were mistaken.' / 'In fact, I was hoping you wouldn't come back.'
jokes.setup: David returns to Stevie after telling her he had feelings for her; he's testing whether she cares
jokes.punchline: Stevie's escalating denials — she didn't call, people were wrong, and in fact she hoped he wouldn't return — are a defensive wall delivered with perfect deadpan
jokes.explanation: The escalating denial structure — each line more dismissive than the last — is well-constructed, and Stevie's delivery makes it feel simultaneously funny and emotionally true (she's clearly protecting herself). 'In fact, I was hoping you wouldn't come back' as the ultimate step in the sequence is both the funniest and most revealing line.

jokes.text: David: 'Wow, believe it or not, that is one of the warmest welcomes I have received so far!'
jokes.setup: Stevie has told David she was hoping he wouldn't come back
jokes.punchline: David's deadpan receipt of this as a 'warm welcome' — implying his other welcomes were somehow worse
jokes.explanation: David's wounded-but-dry 'warmest welcome I've received so far' implies a ranking of unwelcoming receptions — a small but sharp character joke that acknowledges his social situation without self-pity.

jokes.text: David's explanation for not staying away: 'I was intending on staying away for much longer, but I ran out of money, and forgot my phone charger.'
jokes.setup: Stevie sarcastically notes that David's stated intention of staying away was 'well-intentioned'
jokes.punchline: David confirms he meant to stay gone longer but was defeated by two extremely mundane practical problems — money and a phone charger
jokes.explanation: Excellent character reveal. The juxtaposition of dramatic runaway gesture vs. 'forgot my phone charger' perfectly captures David's inability to sustain grand gestures. The phone charger specifically is funnier than money alone — it implies he'd have found a way to manage if he could just have stayed connected.

jokes.text: David: 'I thought, well, there goes my one friend.' / 'Try saying that one out loud, it is very dark.'
jokes.setup: David is explaining how he felt when he thought he'd lost Stevie after confessing feelings
jokes.punchline: He self-narrates the darkness of his situation — 'try saying that one out loud, it is very dark' — breaking from the emotional moment to comment on his own line
jokes.explanation: A meta-self-referential joke that works because it's emotionally earned — David acknowledging the genuine bleakness of having only one friend, then catching himself and labeling it 'very dark' is both funny and humanizing. It's the episode's warmest comedic moment because it lets David be both self-aware and vulnerable simultaneously.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Okay, well, let's start by you leaving my office, and we'll see where that takes us.' — as her version of 'let's work through this'
jokes.setup: Stevie has said they'll 'work through' the situation with David
jokes.punchline: Her first step for 'working through it' is for David to leave immediately — the most Stevie possible interpretation of progress
jokes.explanation: Stevie's deadpan pragmatism — 'let's work through this' immediately means 'leave' — is perfect character comedy. 'And we'll see where that takes us' as an optimistic tag on 'get out' is the extra layer.

jokes.text: David tries to leave Stevie's office and keeps narrating it: 'I'm gonna leave your office. I'm gonna leave your — I'm gonna leave your office.'
jokes.setup: Stevie has told David to leave; he moves toward the door
jokes.punchline: David cannot leave without narrating and re-narrating the act of leaving — a verbal loop that makes a simple exit comically impossible
jokes.explanation: David's inability to exit gracefully — the verbal spiraling of 'I'm gonna leave your office' repeated three times — is physical comedy rendered through dialogue. His social anxiety manifested as a verbal tic is deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: David's parting words: 'Best wishes to you.' / 'Warmest regards.'
jokes.setup: David is finally leaving Stevie's office after his awkward exit spiral
jokes.punchline: Both characters switch to formal email-sign-off language — 'Best wishes to you' / 'Warmest regards' — as a way to end the charged scene
jokes.explanation: A perfect comedic button on the David/Stevie scene. The pivot to corporate email valedictions — 'Best wishes to you' / 'Warmest regards' — is a brilliant deflation of all the emotional tension that preceded it. Both characters adopting formal office language to close an intimate moment is absurdist and perfectly executed. Very high economy — four words each, completely lands. A genuinely memorable Schitt's Creek exchange.

jokes.text: Moira about the missing persons flyer: 'This was before my nose job, how was anyone supposed to recognize me?'
jokes.setup: Moira is looking at a missing persons flyer that was circulated for David
jokes.punchline: Her concern is not about David but about the unflattering pre-surgery photo of herself used in the flyer
jokes.explanation: A small but rich Moira joke — her objection to the flyer is vanity-based and pre-surgical. The idea that she had a nose job and considers this a meaningful identity discontinuity is perfectly Moira. 'How was anyone supposed to recognize me?' as a genuine concern is beautifully self-absorbed.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Oh David, it's a rare gift to strip vanity of its charm, yet here you are.'
jokes.setup: David is looking at the flyer and presumably being embarrassed by his own photo
jokes.punchline: Johnny's backhanded compliment — David has achieved something unusual: being vain without any of the charm that usually softens vanity
jokes.explanation: Johnny's rare cutting remark — usually the straight man, here he delivers a precise insult. 'Rare gift to strip vanity of its charm' is an elegantly constructed put-down that requires unpacking, which makes it funnier for those who catch it.

jokes.text: Roland's entrance: 'David! I'm so glad to see you back home, all safe and sound.' — followed immediately by 'And I don't want you to worry, I'm not gonna press charges, okay?'
jokes.setup: Roland greets David warmly, with apparent relief
jokes.punchline: The warmth immediately gives way to a 'I'm not pressing charges' caveat — Roland's generosity is transactional and the threat was real
jokes.explanation: A clean misdirection — Roland's warmth is genuine but the 'I'm not gonna press charges' immediately undercuts it. The structure is tight: the longer the warm preamble, the better the legal caveat lands.

jokes.text: Alexis's 'Ooh, burn, David!' after Roland says he won't press charges
jokes.setup: Roland has delivered his not-pressing-charges line
jokes.punchline: Alexis's cheerful, completely misread 'Ooh, burn, David!' — she's treating a legal threat as a playground taunt
jokes.explanation: Alexis's misreading of the social stakes — treating a near-legal threat as a 'burn' — is consistent character comedy, though it's a lighter beat.

jokes.text: David's extended, painful apology — 'Uh...!' of reluctance before every sentence, long pauses, and 'I feel really bad about it.' followed by Roland: 'But do you, David?'
jokes.setup: Johnny tells David to apologize to Roland for stealing his truck; David has to actually do it
jokes.punchline: The apology is a masterclass in reluctant sincerity — each word costs David something — and Roland's 'But do you, David?' perfectly skewers the performance
jokes.explanation: The extended apology sequence is excellent cringe comedy — David's visible agony at each word, the long pauses, and the finally-delivered 'I feel really bad about it' are all funny. But Roland's 'But do you, David?' is the perfect button: he's not wrong to question it, and his reading of David's performance is hilariously accurate.

jokes.text: After the apology is accepted, David immediately takes Roland's juice: 'Okay, I'm taking your juice.' / 'David!'
jokes.setup: David has just completed a painful apology and been forgiven
jokes.punchline: The instant he's off the hook, David reverts — taking Roland's juice without asking, prompting an exasperated 'David!' from Johnny
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect comedic button. The structural elegance is outstanding — David endures the apology scene, is released from it, and within seconds proves that nothing has changed by casually taking Roland's juice. 'David!' as the final word of the episode's main plot is both a great button and a perfect expression of the show's warmth: nothing changes, but somehow everything is okay. Very high character integration and economy.

jokes.text: 'Okay, now let's talk about gas. I'm thinking thirty cents on the mile.' / 'I think we're done here, Roland!'
jokes.setup: Johnny has just declared the apology accepted and the matter settled
jokes.punchline: Roland immediately pivots to billing David for gas mileage — the reconciliation was conditional on payment all along
jokes.explanation: Good Roland button to the apology scene. The instant pivot to gas money reveals the whole magnanimous forgiveness was partly transactional. Johnny's immediate shutdown is the right response and a good scene-ender.

jokes.text: Final scene: Moira teaching Johnny her mother's recipe; he's lost in the steps. 'We've already done that.' / 'What number are we on?' / 'Oh my God!'
jokes.setup: Moira is passing on a family recipe to Johnny in what seems like a wholesome closing scene
jokes.punchline: Johnny has already lost track of the steps; Moira's 'Oh my God!' is the episode's final joke — the two of them unable to successfully collaborate on even a recipe
jokes.explanation: A lighter closing beat — the warmth of the show's domestic core with a small comedic friction. Not the sharpest joke but functions as a cozy episode-ender. Below-average score reflects that it's functional but not inspired.

tmdb_overview: The Roses search for their missing son, David, while Alexis takes her relationship with Mutt to the next level despite technically being engaged to Ted.
insight: David's deadpan indifference carries a season-opener that finds the show's comedic rhythm at 2.7 jokes per minute.
